The Italian [*Caio Duilio*](/wiki/Italian_destroyer_Caio_Duilio "Italian destroyer Caio Duilio"), which belongs to the [Horizon class](/wiki/Horizon-class_frigate "Horizon-class frigate") of Franco-Italian designed first-rate frigates

In [naval](/wiki/Navy "Navy") terminology, a **destroyer** is a fast, maneuverable, long-endurance [warship](/wiki/Warship "Warship") intended to escort
larger vessels in a [fleet](/wiki/Naval_fleet "Naval fleet"), [convoy](/wiki/Convoy "Convoy"), or [carrier battle group](/wiki/Carrier_battle_group "Carrier battle group") and defend them against a wide range of general threats. They were conceived in the late 19th century as a defense against [torpedo boats](/wiki/Torpedo_boat "Torpedo boat"), and by the time of the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") in 1904, these "**torpedo boat destroyers**" (**TBDs**) were "large, swift, and powerfully armed torpedo boats designed to destroy other torpedo boats".[[1]](#cite_note-1) Although the term "destroyer" had been used interchangeably with "TBD" and "torpedo boat destroyer" by navies since 1892, the term "torpedo boat destroyer" was generally shortened to simply "destroyer" by nearly all navies by the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), destroyers were light vessels with little endurance for unattended ocean operations;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] typically, a number of destroyers and a single [destroyer tender](/wiki/Destroyer_tender "Destroyer tender") operated together.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After the war, destroyers grew in size. The American [*Allen M. Sumner*-class destroyers](/wiki/Allen_M._Sumner-class_destroyer "Allen M. Sumner-class destroyer") (from 1943) had a displacement of 2,200 tons, while the [*Arleigh Burke* class](/wiki/Arleigh_Burke-class_destroyer "Arleigh Burke-class destroyer") (from 1991) has a displacement of up to 9,600 tons, a difference of nearly 340%. Moreover, the advent of guided missiles allowed destroyers to take on the surface-combatant roles previously filled by [battleships](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") and [cruisers](/wiki/Cruiser "Cruiser"). This resulted in larger and more powerful [guided-missile destroyers](/wiki/Guided-missile_destroyer "Guided-missile destroyer") more capable of independent operation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the start of the 21st century, destroyers are the global standard for [surface-combatant](/wiki/Surface_combatant "Surface combatant") ships, with only two nations (the [United States](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy")) officially operating the heavier cruisers, with no battleships or true [battlecruisers](/wiki/Battlecruiser "Battlecruiser") remaining.[[note 1]](#cite_note-3) Modern guided-missile destroyers are equivalent in [tonnage](/wiki/Tonnage "Tonnage") but vastly superior in firepower to cruisers of the World War II era, and are capable of carrying [nuclear-tipped](/wiki/Nuclear_missile "Nuclear missile") [cruise missiles](/wiki/Cruise_missile "Cruise missile"). At 510 feet (160 m) long, a displacement of 9,200 tons, and with an armament of more than 90 missiles,[[3]](#cite_note-4) guided-missile destroyers such as the *Arleigh Burke* class are actually larger and more heavily armed than most previous ships classified as guided-missile cruisers. The Chinese [Type 055 destroyer](/wiki/Type_055_destroyer "Type 055 destroyer") has been described as a cruiser in some US Navy reports due to its size and armament.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Many [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") navies, such as the [French](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Navy "Dutch Navy"), [Danish](/wiki/Royal_Danish_Navy "Royal Danish Navy"), and [German](/wiki/German_Navy "German Navy"), use the term "[frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate")" for their destroyers, which leads to some confusion.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Fernando_Villaamil_%28ca_1897%29.jpg/250px-Fernando_Villaamil_%28ca_1897%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fernando_Villaamil_(ca_1897).jpg)

[Fernando Villaamil](/wiki/Fernando_Villaamil "Fernando Villaamil"), credited as the inventor of the destroyer concept, who died in action during the [Battle of Santiago de Cuba](/wiki/Battle_of_Santiago_de_Cuba "Battle of Santiago de Cuba") in 1898.

The emergence and development of the destroyer was related to the invention of the [self-propelled torpedo](/wiki/Self-propelled_torpedo "Self-propelled torpedo") in the 1860s. A navy now had the potential to destroy a superior enemy battle fleet using steam [launches](/wiki/Launch_(boat) "Launch (boat)") to fire torpedoes. Cheap, fast boats armed with torpedoes, called [torpedo boats](/wiki/Torpedo_boat "Torpedo boat"), were built and became a threat to large capital ships near enemy coasts. The first seagoing vessel designed to launch the self-propelled [Whitehead torpedo](/wiki/Whitehead_torpedo "Whitehead torpedo") was the 33-ton [HMS *Lightning*](/wiki/HMS_Lightning_(1876) "HMS Lightning (1876)") in 1876.[[5]](#cite_note-ast-6) She was armed with two drop collars to launch these weapons; these were replaced in 1879 by a single [torpedo tube](/wiki/Torpedo_tube "Torpedo tube") in the bow. By the 1880s, the type had evolved into small ships of 50–100 tons, fast enough to evade enemy picket boats.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At first, the threat of a torpedo-boat attack to a battle fleet was considered to exist only when at anchor, but as faster and longer-range torpedo boats and torpedoes were developed, the threat extended to cruising at sea. In response to this new threat, more heavily gunned picket boats called "catchers" were built, which were used to escort the battle fleet at sea. They needed significant seaworthiness and endurance to operate with the battle fleet, and as they inherently became larger, they became officially designated "torpedo-boat destroyers", and by the First World War were largely known as "destroyers" in English. The antitorpedo boat origin of this type of ship is retained in its name in other languages, including [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (*contre-torpilleur*), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (*cacciatorpediniere*), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") (*contratorpedeiro*), [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") (*torpédoborec*), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") (*antitorpiliko*, αντιτορπιλικό), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") (*torpedobootjager*) and, up until the Second World War, [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") (*kontrtorpedowiec*, now obsolete).[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Once destroyers became more than just catchers guarding an anchorage, they were recognized to be also ideal to take over the offensive role of torpedo boats themselves, so they were also fitted with torpedo tubes in addition to their antitorpedo-boat guns. At that time, and even into World War I, the only function of destroyers was to protect their own battle fleet from enemy torpedo attacks and to make such attacks on the battleships of the enemy. The task of escorting merchant convoys was still in the future.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Early designs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early designs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Kotaka.jpg/250px-Kotaka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kotaka.jpg)

The [Imperial Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy")'s *Kotaka* (1887)

An important development came with the construction of [HMS *Swift*](/wiki/HMS_TB_81_(1885) "HMS TB 81 (1885)") in 1884, later redesignated TB 81.[[7]](#cite_note-8) This was a large (137 ton) torpedo boat with four [47 mm quick-firing guns](/wiki/QF_3-pounder_Hotchkiss "QF 3-pounder Hotchkiss") and three torpedo tubes. At 23.75 [knots](/wiki/Knot_(unit) "Knot (unit)") (43.99 km/h; 27.33 mph), while still not fast enough to engage enemy torpedo boats reliably, the ship at least had the armament to deal with them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another forerunner of the torpedo-boat destroyer (TBD) was the Japanese torpedo boat[[8]](#cite_note-9) [*Kotaka*](/wiki/Japanese_torpedo_boat_Kotaka "Japanese torpedo boat Kotaka") (*Falcon*), built in 1885.[[9]](#cite_note-10) Designed to Japanese specifications and ordered from the Isle of Dogs, London [Yarrow shipyard](/wiki/Yarrow_shipyards "Yarrow shipyards") in 1885, she was transported in parts to Japan, where she was assembled and launched in 1887. The 165-foot (50 m) long vessel was armed with four 1-pounder (37 mm) quick-firing guns and six [torpedo](/wiki/Torpedo "Torpedo") tubes, reached 19 knots (35 km/h), and at 203 tons, was the largest torpedo boat built to date. In her trials in 1889, *Kotaka* demonstrated that she could exceed the role of coastal defense, and was capable of accompanying larger [warships](/wiki/Warship "Warship") on the high seas. The Yarrow shipyards, builder of the parts for *Kotaka*, "considered Japan to have effectively invented the destroyer".[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The German [aviso](/wiki/Aviso "Aviso") [*Greif*](/wiki/SMS_Greif_(1886) "SMS Greif (1886)"), launched in 1886, was designed as a "*Torpedojäger*" (torpedo hunter), intended to screen the fleet against attacks by torpedo boats. The ship was significantly larger than torpedo boats of the period, displacing some 2,266 t (2,230 long tons), with an armament of 10.5 cm (4.1 in) guns and 3.7 cm (1.5 in) [Hotchkiss revolver cannon](/wiki/Hotchkiss_revolver_cannon "Hotchkiss revolver cannon").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

### Torpedo gunboat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Torpedo gunboat")]

Main article: [Torpedo gunboat](/wiki/Torpedo_gunboat "Torpedo gunboat")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/HMS_Spider.png/250px-HMS_Spider.png)](/wiki/File:HMS_Spider.png)

HMS *Spider*, an early model of torpedo gunboat

The first vessel designed for the explicit purpose of hunting and destroying torpedo boats was the [torpedo gunboat](/wiki/Torpedo_gunboat "Torpedo gunboat"). Essentially very small cruisers, torpedo gunboats were equipped with torpedo tubes and an adequate gun armament, intended for hunting down smaller enemy boats. By the end of the 1890s, torpedo gunboats were made obsolete by their more successful contemporaries, the TBDs, which were much faster.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first example of this was [HMS *Rattlesnake*](/wiki/HMS_Rattlesnake_(1886) "HMS Rattlesnake (1886)"), designed by [Nathaniel Barnaby](/wiki/Nathaniel_Barnaby "Nathaniel Barnaby") in 1885, and commissioned in response to the [Russian War scare](/wiki/Panjdeh_Incident "Panjdeh Incident").[[12]](#cite_note-L&W-13) The gunboat was armed with torpedoes and designed for hunting and destroying smaller [torpedo boats](/wiki/Torpedo_boat "Torpedo boat"). Exactly 200 feet (61 m) long and 23 feet (7.0 m) in beam, she displaced 550 tons. Built of steel, *Rattlesnake* was unarmoured with the exception of a 3⁄4-inch protective deck. She was armed with a single [4-inch/25-pounder breech-loading gun](/wiki/BL_4_inch_naval_gun_Mk_I_%E2%80%93_VI "BL 4 inch naval gun Mk I – VI"), six [3-pounder QF guns](/wiki/QF_3_pounder_Hotchkiss "QF 3 pounder Hotchkiss") and four 14-inch (360 mm) torpedo tubes, arranged with two fixed tubes at the bow and a set of torpedo-dropping carriages on either side. Four torpedo reloads were carried.[[12]](#cite_note-L&W-13)

A number of torpedo gunboat classes followed, including the *Grasshopper* class, the [*Sharpshooter* class](/wiki/Sharpshooter-class_torpedo_gunboat "Sharpshooter-class torpedo gunboat"), the [*Alarm* class](/wiki/Alarm-class_torpedo_gunboat "Alarm-class torpedo gunboat"), and the [*Dryad* class](/wiki/Dryad-class_torpedo_gunboat "Dryad-class torpedo gunboat") – all built for the Royal Navy during the 1880s and the 1890s. In the 1880s, the [Chilean Navy](/wiki/Chilean_Navy "Chilean Navy") ordered the construction of two [*Almirante Lynch* class](/wiki/Almirante_Lynch-class_torpedo_gunboat "Almirante Lynch-class torpedo gunboat") torpedo gunboats from the British shipyard Laird Brothers, which specialized in the construction of this type of vessel. The novelty is that one of these *Almirante Lynch*-class torpedo boats managed to sink the ironclad [*Blanco Encalada*](/wiki/Chilean_ironclad_Blanco_Encalada "Chilean ironclad Blanco Encalada") with self-propelled torpedoes in the [Battle of Caldera Bay](/wiki/Battle_of_Caldera_Bay "Battle of Caldera Bay") in 1891, thus surpassing its main function of hunting torpedo boats.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Fernando Villaamil](/wiki/Fernando_Villaamil "Fernando Villaamil"), second officer of the Ministry of the [Navy of Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy"), designed his own torpedo gunboat to combat the threat from the torpedo boat.[[13]](#cite_note-14) He asked several British shipyards to submit proposals capable of fulfilling these specifications. In 1885, the Spanish Navy chose the design submitted by the shipyard of James and [George Thomson](/wiki/George_Thomson_(shipbuilder) "George Thomson (shipbuilder)") of [Clydebank](/wiki/Clydebank "Clydebank"). [*Destructor*](/wiki/Spanish_warship_Destructor "Spanish warship Destructor") (*Destroyer* in Spanish) was laid down at the end of the year, launched in 1886, and commissioned in 1887. Some authors considered her as the first destroyer ever built.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Contratorpedero_Destructor_%28en_1890%29.svg/250px-Contratorpedero_Destructor_%28en_1890%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Contratorpedero_Destructor_(en_1890).svg)

Spanish warship [*Destructor*](/wiki/Spanish_warship_Destructor "Spanish warship Destructor") (*Destroyer*), 1890

She displaced 348 tons, and was the first warship[[16]](#cite_note-17) equipped with twin [triple-expansion engines](/wiki/Triple-expansion_engine "Triple-expansion engine") generating 3,784 ihp (2,822 kW), for a maximum speed of 22.6 knots (41.9 km/h),[[17]](#cite_note-dest-18) which made her one of the faster ships in the world in 1888.[[18]](#cite_note-19) She was armed with one 90 mm (3.5 in) Spanish-designed [Hontoria](/wiki/Hontoria "Hontoria") [breech-loading](/wiki/Breech-loading_weapon "Breech-loading weapon") gun,[[19]](#cite_note-Bernie2-20) four 57 mm (2.2 in) ([6-pounder](/wiki/QF_6_pounder_Nordenfelt "QF 6 pounder Nordenfelt")) [Nordenfelt](/wiki/Thorsten_Nordenfelt "Thorsten Nordenfelt") guns, two 37 mm (1.5 in) (3-pdr) [Hotchkiss](/wiki/Hotchkiss_et_Cie "Hotchkiss et Cie") cannons and two 15-inch (38 cm) [Schwartzkopff torpedo](/wiki/Schwartzkopff_torpedo "Schwartzkopff torpedo") tubes.[[17]](#cite_note-dest-18) The ship carried three torpedoes per tube.[[19]](#cite_note-Bernie2-20) She carried a crew of 60.[[17]](#cite_note-dest-18)

In terms of gunnery, dimensions, the specialised design and speed to chase torpedo boats, and her high-seas capabilities, *Destructor* was a highly influential precursor to the TBD.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-Bernie2-20)

## Development of the destroyer class

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development of the destroyer class")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/HMS_Havock_%281893%29.jpg/250px-HMS_Havock_%281893%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Havock_(1893).jpg)

[HMS *Havock*](/wiki/HMS_Havock_(1893) "HMS Havock (1893)"), the first ship formally designated as destroyer class, 1894

The first classes of ships to bear the formal designation TBD were the [*Daring* class](/wiki/Daring-class_destroyer_(1893) "Daring-class destroyer (1893)") of two ships and [*Havock* class](/wiki/Havock-class_destroyer "Havock-class destroyer") of two ships of the Royal Navy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Early torpedo gunboat designs lacked the range and speed to keep up with the fleet they were supposed to protect. In 1892, the [Third Sea Lord](/wiki/Third_Sea_Lord "Third Sea Lord"), [Rear Admiral](/wiki/Rear_Admiral "Rear Admiral") [John "Jacky" Fisher](/wiki/John_Fisher,_1st_Baron_Fisher "John Fisher, 1st Baron Fisher") ordered the development of a new type of ships equipped with the then-novel [water-tube boilers](/wiki/Water-tube_boiler "Water-tube boiler") and [quick-firing](/wiki/British_ordnance_terms#QF "British ordnance terms") small-calibre guns. Six ships to the specifications circulated by the admiralty were ordered initially, comprising three different designs each produced by a different shipbuilder: [HMS *Daring*](/wiki/HMS_Daring_(1893) "HMS Daring (1893)") and [HMS *Decoy*](/wiki/HMS_Decoy_(1894) "HMS Decoy (1894)") from [John I. Thornycroft & Company](/wiki/John_I._Thornycroft_%26_Company "John I. Thornycroft & Company"), [HMS *Havock*](/wiki/HMS_Havock_(1893) "HMS Havock (1893)") and [HMS *Hornet*](/wiki/HMS_Hornet_(1893) "HMS Hornet (1893)") from [Yarrows](/wiki/Yarrow_Shipbuilders "Yarrow Shipbuilders"), and [HMS *Ferret*](/wiki/HMS_Ferret_(1893) "HMS Ferret (1893)") and [HMS *Lynx*](/wiki/HMS_Lynx_(1894) "HMS Lynx (1894)") from [Laird, Son & Company](/wiki/Cammell_Laird "Cammell Laird").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

These ships all featured a turtleback (i.e. rounded) [forecastle](/wiki/Forecastle "Forecastle") that was characteristic of early British TBDs. [HMS *Daring*](/wiki/HMS_Daring_(1893) "HMS Daring (1893)") and [HMS *Decoy*](/wiki/HMS_Decoy_(1894) "HMS Decoy (1894)") were both built by [Thornycroft](/wiki/Thornycroft "Thornycroft"), displaced 260 tons (287.8 tons full load), and were 185 feet in length. They were armed with one [12-pounder gun](/wiki/QF_12_pounder_12_cwt_naval_gun "QF 12 pounder 12 cwt naval gun") and three 6-pounder guns, with one fixed 18-in torpedo tube in the bow plus two more torpedo tubes on a revolving mount abaft the two funnels. Later, the bow torpedo tube was removed and two more 6-pounder guns added, instead. They produced 4,200 hp from a pair of Thornycroft water-tube boilers, giving them a top speed of 27 knots, giving the range and speed to travel effectively with a battle fleet. In common with subsequent early Thornycroft boats, they had sloping sterns and double rudders.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The French navy, an extensive user of torpedo boats, built its first TBD in 1899, with the [*Durandal*-class](/wiki/Durandal-class_destroyer "Durandal-class destroyer") *torpilleur d'escadre*. The United States commissioned its first TBD, [USS *Bainbridge*](/wiki/USS_Bainbridge_(DD-1) "USS Bainbridge (DD-1)"), Destroyer No. 1, in 1902, and by 1906, 16 destroyers were in service with the US Navy.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Subsequent improvements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Subsequent improvements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Yarrow_plan.png/250px-Yarrow_plan.png)](/wiki/File:Yarrow_plan.png)

Builders' plans for the British [*Charger* class](/wiki/Charger-class_destroyer "Charger-class destroyer"), built 1894–95

Torpedo boat destroyer designs continued to evolve around the turn of the 20th century in several key ways. The first was the introduction of the [steam turbine](/wiki/Steam_turbine "Steam turbine"). The spectacular unauthorized demonstration of the turbine-powered [*Turbinia*](/wiki/Turbinia "Turbinia") at the 1897 Spithead Navy Review, which, significantly, was of torpedo-boat size, prompted the Royal Navy to order a prototype turbine-powered destroyer, [HMS *Viper*](/wiki/HMS_Viper_(1899) "HMS Viper (1899)") of 1899. This was the first turbine warship of any kind, and achieved a remarkable 34 knots (63 km/h; 39 mph) on sea trials. By 1910, the turbine had been widely adopted by all navies for their faster ships.[[5]](#cite_note-ast-6)

The second development was the replacement of the torpedo boat-style turtleback foredeck by a raised forecastle for the new [River-class](/wiki/River-class_destroyer_(1903) "River-class destroyer (1903)") destroyers built in 1903, which provided better sea-keeping and more space below deck.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first warship to use only [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") propulsion was the Royal Navy's TBD [HMS *Spiteful*](/wiki/HMS_Spiteful_(1899) "HMS Spiteful (1899)"), after experiments in 1904, although the obsolescence of coal as a fuel in British warships was delayed by oil's availability.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) Other navies also adopted oil, for instance the USN with the [*Paulding* class](/wiki/Paulding-class_destroyer "Paulding-class destroyer") of 1909.
In spite of all this variety, destroyers adopted a largely similar pattern. The hull was long and narrow, with a relatively shallow draft. The bow was either raised in a forecastle or covered under a turtleback; underneath this were the crew spaces, extending 1⁄4 to 1⁄3 the way along the hull. Aft of the crew spaces was as much engine space as the technology of the time would allow - several boilers and engines or turbines. Above deck, one or more quick-firing guns were mounted in the bows, in front of the bridge; several more were mounted amidships and astern. Two tube mountings (later on, multiple mountings) were generally found amidships.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Between 1892 and 1914, destroyers became markedly larger; initially 275 tons with a length of 165 feet (50 m) for the Royal Navy's first [*Havock* class](/wiki/Havock-class_destroyer "Havock-class destroyer") of TBDs,[[26]](#cite_note-27) up to the First World War with 300-foot (91 m) long destroyers displacing 1,000 tons was not unusual. Construction remained focused on putting the biggest possible engines into a small hull, though, resulting in a somewhat flimsy construction. Often, hulls were built of [high-tensile steel](/wiki/High-tensile_steel "High-tensile steel")[[5]](#cite_note-ast-6) only 1⁄8 in (3.2 mm) thick.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By 1910, the steam-driven displacement (that is, not [hydroplaning](/wiki/Planing_(sailing) "Planing (sailing)")) torpedo boat had become redundant as a separate type. Germany, nevertheless, continued to build such boats until the end of World War I, although these were effectively small coastal destroyers. In fact, Germany never distinguished between the two types, giving them pennant numbers in the same series and never giving names to destroyers. Ultimately, the term "torpedo boat" came to be attached to a quite different vessel – the very fast-hydroplaning, motor-driven [motor torpedo boat](/wiki/Motor_torpedo_boat "Motor torpedo boat").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Early use and World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early use and World War I")]

Navies originally built TBDrs to protect against torpedo boats, but admirals soon appreciated the flexibility of the fast, multipurpose vessels that resulted. Vice-Admiral Sir [Baldwin Walker](/wiki/Baldwin_Walker "Baldwin Walker") laid down destroyer duties for the Royal Navy:[[27]](#cite_note-28)

* Screening the advance of a fleet when hostile torpedo craft are about
* Searching a hostile coast along which a fleet might pass
* Watching an enemy's port for the purpose of harassing his torpedo craft and preventing their return
* Attacking an enemy fleet

Early destroyers were extremely cramped places to live, being "without a doubt magnificent fighting vessels ... but unable to stand bad weather".[[28]](#cite_note-29) During the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") in 1904, the commander of the Imperial Japanese Navy TBD *Akatsuki*[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) described "being in command of a destroyer for a long period, especially in wartime ... is not very good for the health". Stating that he had originally been strong and healthy, he continued, "life on a destroyer in winter, with bad food, no comforts, would sap the powers of the strongest men in the long run. A *destroyer* is always more uncomfortable than the others, and rain, snow, and *sea-water* combine to make them damp; in fact, in bad weather, there is not a dry spot where one can rest for a moment."[[32]](#cite_note-Grant-33)

The Japanese destroyer-commander finished with, "Yesterday, I looked at myself in a mirror for a long time; I was disagreeably surprised to see my face thin, full of wrinkles, and as old as though I were 50. My clothes (uniform) cover nothing but a skeleton, and my bones are full of [rheumatism](/wiki/Rheumatism "Rheumatism")."[[32]](#cite_note-Grant-33)

In 1898, the US Navy officially classified [USS *Porter*](/wiki/USS_Porter_(TB-6) "USS Porter (TB-6)"), a 175-foot (53 m) long all steel vessel displacing 165 tons, as a torpedo boat, but her commander, LT. John C. Fremont, described her as "a compact mass of machinery not meant to keep the sea nor to live in ... as five-sevenths of the ship are taken up by machinery and fuel, whilst the remaining two-sevenths, fore and aft, are the crew's quarters; officers forward and the men placed aft. And even in those spaces are placed anchor engines, steering engines, steam pipes, etc. rendering them unbearably hot in tropical regions."[[33]](#cite_note-34)

### Early combat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early combat")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/HMS_Loyal_%281913%29_IWM_SP_001136.jpg/250px-HMS_Loyal_%281913%29_IWM_SP_001136.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Loyal_(1913)_IWM_SP_001136.jpg)

[HMS *Loyal*](/wiki/HMS_Loyal_(1913) "HMS Loyal (1913)"), of the [*Laforey* class](/wiki/Laforey-class_destroyer_(1913) "Laforey-class destroyer (1913)")

The TBD's first major use in combat came during the [Japanese surprise attack on the Russian fleet](/wiki/Battle_of_Port_Arthur "Battle of Port Arthur") anchored in [Port Arthur](/wiki/L%C3%BCshunkou_District "Lüshunkou District") at the opening of the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") on 8 February 1904.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Three destroyer divisions attacked the Russian fleet in port, firing a total of 18 torpedoes, but only two Russian battleships, [*Tsesarevich*](/wiki/Russian_battleship_Tsesarevich "Russian battleship Tsesarevich") and [*Retvizan*](/wiki/Russian_battleship_Retvizan "Russian battleship Retvizan"), and a [protected cruiser](/wiki/Protected_cruiser "Protected cruiser"), [*Pallada*](/wiki/Russian_cruiser_Pallada_(1899) "Russian cruiser Pallada (1899)"), were seriously damaged due to the proper deployment of [torpedo nets](/wiki/Torpedo_net "Torpedo net"). *Tsesarevich*, the Russian flagship, had her nets deployed, with at least four enemy torpedoes "hung up" in them,[[34]](#cite_note-35) and other warships were similarly saved from further damage by their nets.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

While capital-ship engagements were scarce in World War I, destroyer units engaged almost continually in raiding and patrol actions. The first shot of the war at sea was fired on 5 August 1914 by [HMS *Lance*](/wiki/HMS_Lance_(1914) "HMS Lance (1914)"), one of the [3rd Destroyer Flotilla](/wiki/3rd_Destroyer_Flotilla "3rd Destroyer Flotilla"), in an engagement with the German auxiliary [minelayer](/wiki/Minelayer "Minelayer") [*Königin Luise*](/wiki/SS_K%C3%B6nigin_Luise_(1913) "SS Königin Luise (1913)").[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Destroyers were involved in the skirmishes that prompted the [Battle of Heligoland Bight](/wiki/Battle_of_Heligoland_(1914) "Battle of Heligoland (1914)"), and filled a range of roles in the [Battle of Gallipoli](/wiki/Battle_of_Gallipoli "Battle of Gallipoli"), acting as troop transports and as fire-support vessels, as well as their fleet-screening role. Over 80 British destroyers and 60 German torpedo boats took part in the [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland"), which involved pitched small-boat actions between the main fleets, and several foolhardy attacks by unsupported destroyers on capital ships. Jutland also concluded with a messy night action between the German [High Seas Fleet](/wiki/High_Seas_Fleet "High Seas Fleet") and part of the British destroyer screen.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Wickes_dd75.jpg/250px-Wickes_dd75.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wickes_dd75.jpg)

[USS *Wickes*](/wiki/USS_Wickes_(DD-75) "USS Wickes (DD-75)"), a [*Wickes*-class](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer") destroyer

The threat evolved by World War I with the development of the [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine"), or [U-boat](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat"). The submarine had the potential to hide from gunfire and close underwater to fire torpedoes. Early-war destroyers had the speed and armament to intercept submarines before they submerged, either by gunfire or by ramming. Destroyers also had a shallow enough draft that they were difficult to hit with torpedoes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/HMS_Badger_%281911%29.jpg/250px-HMS_Badger_%281911%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Badger_(1911).jpg)

[HMS *Badger*](/wiki/HMS_Badger_(1911) "HMS Badger (1911)") was the first destroyer to successfully ram a submarine.

The desire to attack submarines under water led to rapid destroyer evolution during the war. They were quickly equipped with strengthened bows for ramming, and [depth charges](/wiki/Depth_charge "Depth charge") and [hydrophones](/wiki/Hydrophone "Hydrophone") for identifying submarine targets. The first submarine casualty credited to a destroyer was the German [*U-19*](/wiki/SM_U-19_(Germany) "SM U-19 (Germany)"), rammed by [HMS *Badger*](/wiki/HMS_Badger_(1911) "HMS Badger (1911)") on 29 October 1914. While *U-19* was only damaged, the next month, [HMS *Garry*](/wiki/HMS_Garry "HMS Garry") successfully sank [*U-18*](/wiki/SM_U-18 "SM U-18"). The first depth-charge sinking was on 4 December 1916, when [*UC-19*](/wiki/SM_UC-19 "SM UC-19")[[37]](#cite_note-38) was sunk by HMS *Llewellyn*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The submarine threat meant that many destroyers spent their time on antisubmarine patrol. Once Germany adopted [unrestricted submarine warfare](/wiki/Unrestricted_submarine_warfare "Unrestricted submarine warfare") in January 1917, destroyers were called on to escort merchant [convoys](/wiki/Convoy "Convoy"). US Navy destroyers were among the first American units to be dispatched upon the American entry to the war, and a squadron of Japanese destroyers even joined Allied patrols in the Mediterranean. Patrol duty was far from safe; of the 67 British destroyers lost in the war, collisions accounted for 18, while 12 were wrecked.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the end of the war, the state-of-the-art was represented by the British [W class](/wiki/V_and_W-class_destroyer "V and W-class destroyer").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## 1918–1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1918–1945")]

See also: [List of destroyers of the Second World War](/wiki/List_of_destroyers_of_the_Second_World_War "List of destroyers of the Second World War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Velox.jpg/250px-Velox.jpg)](/wiki/File:Velox.jpg)

V-class destroyer, [HMS *Velox*](/wiki/HMS_Velox_(D34) "HMS Velox (D34)")

The trend during World War I had been towards larger destroyers with heavier armaments. A number of opportunities to fire at capital ships had been missed during the war, because destroyers had expended all their torpedoes in an initial salvo. The British [V and W classes](/wiki/V_and_W-class_destroyer "V and W-class destroyer") of the late war had sought to address this by mounting six torpedo tubes in two triple mounts, instead of the four or two on earlier models. The V and W classes set the standard of destroyer building well into the 1920s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Two Romanian destroyers [*Mărăști*](/wiki/NMS_M%C4%83r%C4%83%C8%99ti "NMS Mărăști") and [*Mărășești*](/wiki/NMS_M%C4%83r%C4%83%C8%99e%C8%99ti "NMS Mărășești"), though, had the greatest firepower of all destroyers in the world throughout the first half of the 1920s. This was largely because, between their commissioning in 1920 and 1926, they retained the armament that they had while serving in the Italian Navy as [scout cruisers](/wiki/Scout_cruiser "Scout cruiser") (*[esploratori](/wiki/Esploratori "Esploratori")*). When initially ordered by Romania in 1913, the Romanian specifications envisioned three 120 mm guns, a caliber which would eventually be adopted as the standard for future Italian destroyers. Armed with three 152 mm and four 76 mm guns after being completed as scout cruisers, the two warships were officially re-rated as destroyers by the [Romanian Navy](/wiki/Romanian_Navy "Romanian Navy"). The two Romanian warships were thus the destroyers with the greatest firepower in the world throughout much of the interwar period. As of 1939, when the Second World War started, their artillery, although changed, was still close to cruiser standards, amounting to nine heavy naval guns (five of 120 mm and four of 76 mm). In addition, they retained their two twin 457 mm torpedo tubes and two machine guns, plus the capacity to carry up to 50 mines.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Uranami_II.jpg/250px-Uranami_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uranami_II.jpg)

*Fubuki*-class destroyer, [*Uranami*](/wiki/Japanese_destroyer_Uranami_(1928) "Japanese destroyer Uranami (1928)")

The next major innovation came with the Japanese [*Fubuki* class](/wiki/Fubuki-class_destroyer "Fubuki-class destroyer") or "special type", designed in 1923 and delivered in 1928. The design was initially noted for its powerful armament of six 5-inch (127 mm) guns and three triple torpedo mounts. The second batch of the class gave the guns high-angle turrets for antiaircraft warfare, and the 24-inch (61 cm), oxygen-fueled Long Lance [Type 93 torpedo](/wiki/Type_93_torpedo "Type 93 torpedo"). The later [*Hatsuharu* class](/wiki/Hatsuharu-class_destroyer "Hatsuharu-class destroyer") of 1931 further improved the torpedo armament by storing its reload torpedoes close at hand in the superstructure, allowing reloading within 15 minutes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Most other nations replied with similar larger ships. The US [*Porter* class](/wiki/Porter-class_destroyer "Porter-class destroyer") adopted twin 5-inch (127 mm) guns, and the subsequent [*Mahan* class](/wiki/Mahan-class_destroyer "Mahan-class destroyer") and [*Gridley* classes](/wiki/Gridley-class_destroyer "Gridley-class destroyer") (the latter of 1934) increased the number of torpedo tubes to 12 and 16, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Fantasque.jpg/250px-Fantasque.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fantasque.jpg)

France's [*Le Fantasque* class](/wiki/Le_Fantasque-class_destroyer "Le Fantasque-class destroyer") is the fastest destroyer class ever built.

In the Mediterranean, the Italian Navy's building of very fast light cruisers of the [*Condottieri* class](/wiki/Condottieri-class_cruiser "Condottieri-class cruiser") prompted the French to produce exceptional destroyer designs. The French had long been keen on large destroyers, with their [*Chacal* class](/wiki/Chacal-class_destroyer "Chacal-class destroyer") of 1922 displacing over 2,000 tons and carrying 130 mm guns; a further three similar classes were produced around 1930. The [*Le Fantasque* class](/wiki/Le_Fantasque-class_destroyer "Le Fantasque-class destroyer") of 1935 carried five 138 millimetres (5.4 in) guns and nine torpedo tubes, but could achieve speeds of 45 knots (83 km/h), which remains the record speed for a steamship and for any destroyer.[[39]](#cite_note-40) The Italians' own destroyers were almost as swift; most Italian designs of the 1930s were rated at over 38 knots (70 km/h), while carrying torpedoes and either four or six 120 mm guns.

Germany started to build destroyers again during the 1930s as part of Hitler's rearmament program. The Germans were also fond of large destroyers, but while the initial [Type 1934](/wiki/German_World_War_II_destroyers "German World War II destroyers") displaced over 3,000 tons, their armament was equal to smaller vessels. This changed from the Type 1936 onwards, which mounted heavy 150 millimetres (5.9 in) guns. German destroyers also used innovative high-pressure steam machinery; while this should have helped their efficiency, it more often resulted in mechanical problems.

[![HNLMS Tjerk Hiddes, a British built N-class destroyer, laid down on 22 May 1940 and transferred to the Royal Netherlands Navy](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Torpedobootjager_Hr.Ms.Tjerk_Hiddes_%28G16%29_%28ex._Non_Pareil%29_onder_Nederlandse_vlag_met_Brits_kenteken_G16_%282158_012325%29.jpg/250px-Torpedobootjager_Hr.Ms.Tjerk_Hiddes_%28G16%29_%28ex._Non_Pareil%29_onder_Nederlandse_vlag_met_Brits_kenteken_G16_%282158_012325%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torpedobootjager_Hr.Ms.Tjerk_Hiddes_(G16)_(ex._Non_Pareil)_onder_Nederlandse_vlag_met_Brits_kenteken_G16_(2158_012325).jpg)

HNLMS *[Tjerk Hiddes](/wiki/HNLMS_Tjerk_Hiddes_(G16) "HNLMS Tjerk Hiddes (G16)")*, a British built N-class destroyer, laid down on 22 May 1940 and transferred to the Royal Netherlands Navy

Once German and Japanese rearmament became clear, the British and American navies consciously focused on building destroyers that were smaller, but more numerous than those used by other nations. The British built a series of destroyers (the [A class](/wiki/A-class_destroyer_(1929) "A-class destroyer (1929)") to [I class](/wiki/I-class_destroyer "I-class destroyer")), which were about 1,400 tons standard displacement, and had four 4.7-inch (119 mm) guns and eight torpedo tubes; the American [*Benson* class](/wiki/Benson-class_destroyer "Benson-class destroyer") of 1938 was similar in size, but carried five 5-inch (127 mm) guns and ten torpedo tubes. Realizing the need for heavier gun armament, the British built the [Tribal class](/wiki/Tribal-class_destroyer_(1936) "Tribal-class destroyer (1936)") of 1936 (sometimes called *Afridi* after one of two lead ships). These ships displaced 1,850 tons and were armed with eight 4.7-inch (119 mm) guns in four twin turrets and four torpedo tubes. These were followed by the [J-class](/wiki/J-,_K-_and_N-class_destroyer "J-, K- and N-class destroyer") and L-class destroyers, with six 4.7-inch (119 mm) guns in twin turrets and eight torpedo tubes.

Antisubmarine sensors included [sonar](/wiki/Sonar "Sonar") (or ASDIC), although training in their use was indifferent. Antisubmarine weapons changed little, and ahead-throwing weapons, a need recognized in World War I, had made no progress.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Later combat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Later combat")]

Further information: [American World War II destroyers](/wiki/American_World_War_II_destroyers "American World War II destroyers"), [British World War II destroyers](/wiki/British_World_War_II_destroyers "British World War II destroyers"), [French World War II destroyers](/wiki/French_World_War_II_destroyers "French World War II destroyers"), [German World War II destroyers](/wiki/German_World_War_II_destroyers "German World War II destroyers"), [Italian World War II destroyers](/wiki/Italian_World_War_II_destroyers "Italian World War II destroyers"), [Japanese World War II destroyers](/wiki/Japanese_World_War_II_destroyers "Japanese World War II destroyers"), [Soviet World War II destroyers](/wiki/Soviet_World_War_II_destroyers "Soviet World War II destroyers"), and [Romanian World War II destroyers](/wiki/Romanian_World_War_II_destroyers "Romanian World War II destroyers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/USS_McGowan_%28DD-678%29_at_sea%2C_circa_in_1945.jpg/250px-USS_McGowan_%28DD-678%29_at_sea%2C_circa_in_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_McGowan_(DD-678)_at_sea,_circa_in_1945.jpg)

[USS *McGowan*](/wiki/USS_McGowan "USS McGowan"), a [*Fletcher*-class](/wiki/Fletcher-class_destroyer "Fletcher-class destroyer") destroyer during World War II

During the 1920s and 1930s, destroyers were often deployed to areas of diplomatic tension or humanitarian disaster. British and American destroyers were common on the Chinese coast and rivers, even supplying landing parties to protect colonial interests. By World War II, the threat had evolved once again. Submarines were more effective, and [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft") had become important weapons of naval warfare; once again the early-war fleet destroyers were ill-equipped for combating these new targets. They were fitted with new light [antiaircraft](/wiki/Antiaircraft "Antiaircraft") guns, [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar"), and [forward-launched ASW](/wiki/Hedgehog_(weapon) "Hedgehog (weapon)") weapons, in addition to their existing [dual-purpose guns](/wiki/Dual-purpose_gun "Dual-purpose gun"), [depth charges](/wiki/Depth_charge "Depth charge"), and torpedoes. Increasing size allowed improved internal arrangement of propulsion machinery with [compartmentation](/wiki/Compartmentation "Compartmentation"), so ships were less likely to be sunk by a single hit.[[5]](#cite_note-ast-6) In most cases torpedo and/or dual-purpose gun armament was reduced to accommodate new [anti-air](/wiki/Anti-air_warfare "Anti-air warfare") and [anti-submarine](/wiki/Anti-submarine_warfare "Anti-submarine warfare") weapons. By this time the destroyers had become large, multi-purpose vessels, expensive targets in their own right. As a result, casualties on destroyers were among the highest. In the US Navy, particularly in World War II, destroyers became known as tin cans due to their light armor compared to battleships and cruisers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The need for large numbers of antisubmarine ships led to the introduction of smaller and cheaper specialized antisubmarine warships called [corvettes](/wiki/Corvette "Corvette") and [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") by the Royal Navy and [destroyer escorts](/wiki/Destroyer_escort "Destroyer escort") by the USN. A similar programme was belatedly started by the Japanese (see [*Matsu*-class](/wiki/Matsu-class_destroyer "Matsu-class destroyer") destroyer). These ships had the size and displacement of the original TBDs from which the contemporary destroyer had evolved.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Post-World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Post-World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/ORP_B%C5%82yskawica_w_nowym_kamufla%C5%BCu_-_02-01-2012.jpg/250px-ORP_B%C5%82yskawica_w_nowym_kamufla%C5%BCu_-_02-01-2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:ORP_B%C5%82yskawica_w_nowym_kamufla%C5%BCu_-_02-01-2012.jpg)

The [Polish](/wiki/Polish_Navy "Polish Navy") destroyer [ORP *Błyskawica*](/wiki/ORP_B%C5%82yskawica "ORP Błyskawica") is preserved as a [museum ship](/wiki/Museum_ship "Museum ship") in [Gdynia](/wiki/Gdynia "Gdynia").

Some conventional destroyers completed in the late 1940s and 1950s were built on wartime experience. These vessels were significantly larger than wartime ships and had fully automatic main guns, unit machinery, radar, sonar, and antisubmarine weapons, such as the [squid mortar](/wiki/Squid_(weapon) "Squid (weapon)"). Examples include the British [*Daring*-class](/wiki/Daring-class_destroyer_(1949) "Daring-class destroyer (1949)"), US [*Forrest Sherman*-class](/wiki/Forrest_Sherman-class_destroyer "Forrest Sherman-class destroyer"), and the Soviet [*Kotlin*-class](/wiki/Kotlin-class_destroyer "Kotlin-class destroyer") destroyers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some World War II–vintage ships were modernized for antisubmarine warfare, and to extend their service lives, to avoid having to build (expensive) brand-new ships. Examples include the US [FRAM I](/wiki/FRAM_I "FRAM I") programme and the British [Type 15 frigates](/wiki/Type_15_frigate "Type 15 frigate") converted from fleet destroyers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The advent of [surface-to-air missiles](/wiki/Surface-to-air_missile "Surface-to-air missile") and [surface-to-surface missiles](/wiki/Surface-to-surface_missile "Surface-to-surface missile"), such as the [Exocet](/wiki/Exocet "Exocet"), in the early 1960s changed naval warfare. [Guided-missile destroyers](/wiki/Guided-missile_destroyer "Guided-missile destroyer") (DDG in the US Navy) were developed to carry these weapons and protect the fleet from air, submarine, and surface threats. Examples include the Soviet [*Kashin* class](/wiki/Kashin-class_destroyer "Kashin-class destroyer"), the British [County class](/wiki/County-class_destroyer "County-class destroyer"), and the US [*Charles F. Adams* class](/wiki/Charles_F._Adams-class_destroyer "Charles F. Adams-class destroyer").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 21st century destroyers tend to display features such as large, slab sides without complicated corners and crevices to keep the [radar cross-section](/wiki/Radar_cross-section "Radar cross-section") small, [vertical launch systems](/wiki/Vertical_launch_system "Vertical launch system") to carry a large number of missiles at high readiness to fire, and [helicopter](/wiki/Helicopter "Helicopter") flight decks and [hangars](/wiki/Hangars "Hangars").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Future development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Destroyer&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Future development")]

* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/05/Flag_of_Brazil.svg/40px-Flag_of_Brazil.svg.png) [Brazilian Navy](/wiki/Brazilian_Navy "Brazilian Navy") plans to build 7,000-ton destroyers after the delivery of the new frigates, and TKMS presented to the Navy its most modern 7,200-ton MEKO A-400 air defense destroyer, an updated version of the German [F-125](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg-class_frigate "Baden-Württemberg-class frigate")-class frigates. The similarities between the projects and the high rate of commonality between requirements were also crucial for the consortium's victory.[[47]](#cite_note-48)
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Naval_ensign_of_Canada.svg/40px-Naval_ensign_of_Canada.svg.png) [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") is building up to 15 [River-class](/wiki/River-class_destroyer_(2030s) "River-class destroyer (2030s)") destroyers based on the Royal Navy's [Type 26 Global Combat Ship](/wiki/Type_26_Global_Combat_Ship "Type 26 Global Combat Ship"). They will be more powerful than the Type 26, being fitted with the [Aegis Combat System](/wiki/Aegis_Combat_System "Aegis Combat System") and long range surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Naval_Ensign_of_China.svg/40px-Naval_Ensign_of_China.svg.png) [People's Liberation Army Navy](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Navy "People's Liberation Army Navy") is adding six more [Type 052D destroyer](/wiki/Type_052D_destroyer "Type 052D destroyer") and sixteen more [Type 055 destroyer](/wiki/Type_055_destroyer "Type 055 destroyer") class ships to its navy.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Ensign_of_France.svg/40px-Ensign_of_France.svg.png) [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy") is building five new [*Amiral Ronarc'h*-class destroyers](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9gate_de_d%C3%A9fense_et_d%27intervention "Frégate de défense et d'intervention") (classed as "first rank frigates" in the French Navy).[[43]](#cite_note-frenchnavy-44)
* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Naval_Ensign_of_Germany.svg/40px-Naval_Ensign_of_Germany.svg.png) [German Navy](/wiki/German_Navy "German Navy"): Six multi-mission [F126 frigates](/wiki/F126_frigate "F126 frigate") which will have destroyer-size and corresponding capabilities (Length: 163 m, displacement: 10,550 tons)[[48]](#cite_note-49)
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Flag_of_Greece.svg/40px-Flag_of_Greece.svg.png) [Hellenic Navy](/wiki/Hellenic_Navy "Hellenic Navy") has ordered three [Frégate de défense et d'intervention](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9gate_de_d%C3%A9fense_et_d%27intervention "Frégate de défense et d'intervention") (with an option on a fourth) from France.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Naval_Ensign_of_India.svg/40px-Naval_Ensign_of_India.svg.png) [Indian Navy](/wiki/Indian_Navy "Indian Navy") has begun development of its [Project-18 Next Generation Destroyers](/wiki/Project_18-class_destroyer "Project 18-class destroyer").[[49]](#cite_note-50)
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Flag_of_Iran.svg/40px-Flag_of_Iran.svg.png) [Islamic Republic of Iran Navy](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Iran_Navy "Islamic Republic of Iran Navy") is building 1-2 [*Khalije Fars*-class](/wiki/Khalije_Fars-class_destroyer "Khalije Fars-class destroyer") destroyers.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Naval_ensign_of_Italy.svg/40px-Naval_ensign_of_Italy.svg.png) [Italian Navy](/wiki/Italian_Navy "Italian Navy") is researching development into their new DDX project to replace their *Durand da le Penne*-class destroyers.[[50]](#cite_note-51)
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Naval_Ensign_of_Japan.svg/40px-Naval_Ensign_of_Japan.svg.png) [Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Maritime_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force") is developing plans for its [DDR Destroyer Revolution](/wiki/DDR_Destroyer_Revolution "DDR Destroyer Revolution") Project.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Flag_of_the_Republic_of_Korea_Navy.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_Republic_of_Korea_Navy.svg.png) [Republic of Korea Navy](/wiki/Republic_of_Korea_Navy "Republic of Korea Navy") has begun development of its [KDX-IIA](/wiki/KDX-IIA "KDX-IIA") destroyers. These ships are to be a subclass of South Korea's [*Chungmugong Yi Sun-shin*-class](/wiki/Chungmugong_Yi_Sun-shin-class_destroyer "Chungmugong Yi Sun-shin-class destroyer") destroyers. The first unit is expected to enter service in 2019. Additionally, [*Sejong the Great*-class](/wiki/Sejong_the_Great-class_destroyer "Sejong the Great-class destroyer") destroyers are being built.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Naval_ensign_of_Russia.svg/40px-Naval_ensign_of_Russia.svg.png) [Russian Navy](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy") has begun development of its [Lider-class](/wiki/Lider-class_destroyer "Lider-class destroyer") destroyer. Design work was ongoing as of 2020.
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Flag_of_Turkey.svg/40px-Flag_of_Turkey.svg.png) [Turkish Naval Forces](/wiki/Turkish_Naval_Forces "Turkish Naval Forces") is currently developing its [TF2000-class destroyer](/wiki/TF2000-class_frigate "TF2000-class frigate") as the largest part of the [MILGEM project](/wiki/MILGEM_project "MILGEM project"). A total of seven ships will be constructed and will specialise in [anti-air warfare](/wiki/Anti-air_warfare "Anti-air warfare").
* The United Kingdom's ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Naval_ensign_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg/40px-Naval_ensign_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png) [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") is in the early stages of developing a [Type 83 destroyer](/wiki/Type_83_destroyer "Type 83 destroyer") design after the unveiling of these plans in the [2021 defence white paper](/wiki/Defence_in_a_Competitive_Age "Defence in a Competitive Age"). The class is projected to replace the [Type 45 destroyer](/wiki/Type_45_destroyer "Type 45 destroyer") fleet beginning in the latter 2030s.[[51]](#cite_note-52)
* The ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png) [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") has 19 additional *Arleigh Burke* destroyers planned or under construction. The new ships will be the upgraded "flight III" version.[[52]](#cite_note-CRS_summary-53) The United States has also started development of its [DDG(X)](/wiki/DDG(X) "DDG(X)") next-generation destroyer project.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Construction of the first ship is expected to start in 2028.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
